findings.justification: Price separator settings (decimal and thousand separators) are sanitized with wp_kses_post() which permits full HTML including script-bearing attributes, whereas a plain text sanitizer like sanitize_text_field() is appropriate for single-character separator values.

When $regular_price or $sale_price are non-numeric strings, they bypass wc_price() and are concatenated directly into the returned HTML markup without any escaping, allowing stored or reflected XSS if caller-supplied values reach this branch.
Non-numeric $from and $to values are inserted verbatim into HTML via sprintf without escaping; an attacker who controls these arguments (e.g. via a crafted product price) can inject arbitrary HTML.

The $country variable is interpolated directly into a PCRE pattern string without preg_quote(), so a country code containing regex metacharacters could alter pattern matching or cause unexpected behavior, though in practice $country is validated upstream against a fixed list.

The function returns the result of apply_filters('woocommerce_coupon_code', $value) with no sanitization whatsoever, meaning any filter hook registered by a third-party plugin can return arbitrary unsanitized content that callers may render in HTML.

The $aria_hidden string fragment is built from $args['aria-hidden'] and concatenated into the HTML return value; because $args is filtered via wc_price_args, a third-party filter replacing this value with a non-boolean could inject content into the span attributes.
